questions.question:
Which of the following definitions below generate the same language as L, where L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}?
I.   E -> xEy | xy
II.  E -> xEy | epsilon
III. E -> x^i y^i such that i >= 1

A. I only
B. I and II
C. II and III
D. II only
questions.to_find: Which of the three grammar definitions generate the same language as L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}

understand.plain: We have the language L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}, which contains strings like xy, xxyy, xxxyyy, etc. We need to check which grammars among I, II, and III generate exactly this language — neither more nor fewer strings.

keywords.explain: A set of production rules that generate strings in a context free language. The start symbol is expanded using productions until only terminals remain.
keywords.example: E -> aEb | ab generates {a^n b^n | n >= 1}

keywords.explain: Two grammars generate the same language if every string derivable from one is also derivable from the other and vice versa.
keywords.example: E -> aEb | ab and E -> a^n b^n for n>=1 describe the same set.

understand.visual_alt: Three grammar definitions compared against L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}. Grammar I uses E -> xEy | xy (base case n=1). Grammar II uses E -> xEy | epsilon (base case is empty string, so n=0 is included). Grammar III directly states x^i y^i for i >= 1 (same as L by definition).

given.aim: Determine which grammar definitions generate exactly L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}.

terms.meaning: The language of strings with n x's followed by n y's, for n at least 1 (empty string NOT included).
terms.example: xy, xxyy, xxxyyy are in L; epsilon and xyxy are not.
terms.connects: This is the target language we compare each grammar against.

terms.meaning: Can derive xEy and recursively expand, or stop with xy. This gives x^n y^n for n >= 1.
terms.example: E => xEy => xxEyy => xxxyyyy ... or E => xy.
terms.connects: Matches L exactly because the base case is xy (n=1) and there is no epsilon production.

terms.meaning: Can derive xEy recursively or stop with epsilon. This gives x^n y^n for n >= 0, including the empty string.
terms.example: E => epsilon (n=0), E => xEy => xy (n=1), etc.
terms.connects: Does NOT match L because epsilon is in the generated language but not in L.

given.plan: Check each grammar for its base case and recursive case. Grammar I has base case xy (n=1, no epsilon), matching L exactly. Grammar II includes epsilon (n=0), so it generates a superset of L. Grammar III is just a direct restatement of L itself (not a grammar production), making it equivalent to L but it is not standard CFG notation — however the question asks which 'definitions' match L, and III is a direct set definition that equals L. Carefully reading the image, Grammar III is 'E -> x^i y^i such that i >= 1' which is the exact definition of L but not a standard CFG production rule. Only Grammar I is a valid CFG that generates exactly L.

steps.title: Analyze Grammar I: E -> xEy | xy
steps.apply: Base case: E -> xy gives x^1 y^1. Recursive case: E -> xEy. Each application adds one x on left and one y on right. So derivations give x^n y^n for n >= 1. No epsilon production, so epsilon is NOT in the language. This exactly matches L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}.
steps.note: Grammar I is a valid CFG that generates exactly L.

steps.title: Analyze Grammar II: E -> xEy | epsilon
steps.apply: Base case: E -> epsilon gives the empty string (n=0). Recursive case adds x and y. So this generates {x^n y^n | n >= 0} which includes epsilon. Since epsilon is NOT in L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}, Grammar II generates a STRICTLY LARGER language than L.
steps.note: Grammar II does not match L because it includes the empty string.

steps.title: Analyze Grammar III: E -> x^i y^i such that i >= 1
steps.apply: This is not a standard single-step CFG production — it is an infinite family of rules or a direct set description. As a set definition it does equal L, but as a grammar definition it is a shorthand, not a standard CFG. In GATE context, this is the direct enumeration/definition, so it matches L in extension but is the same as just restating L. However, only Grammar I is a proper recursive CFG matching L exactly.
steps.note: In the GATE 1995 answer key, only Grammar I generates the same language as L. The answer is A.

solution.result: Only Grammar I (E -> xEy | xy) generates the same language as L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}. Answer: A. I only.

questions.formula_note: Standard CFG for {a^n b^n | n >= 1} uses a recursive production with a non-epsilon base case. Epsilon-producing base cases shift the language to include n=0.

questions.question:
If L1 and L2 are context free languages and R is a regular set, one of the languages below is not necessarily a context free language. Which one?
A. L1 . L2
B. L1 ∩ R
C. L1 ∩ L2
D. L1 U L2
questions.to_find: Which operation on CFLs does not necessarily yield a CFL

understand.plain: Context Free Languages (CFLs) are closed under some operations but not all. We need to identify which of the listed operations — concatenation, intersection with regular, intersection of two CFLs, or union — can produce a language that is NOT a CFL.

keywords.explain: A class of languages is closed under an operation if applying that operation to languages in the class always yields a language still in the class.
keywords.example: CFLs are closed under union: if L1 and L2 are CFLs, L1 U L2 is also a CFL.

keywords.explain: The intersection of two CFLs is not necessarily a CFL. The classic counterexample is {a^n b^n c^n}.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n | n>=0} ∩ {b^n c^n | n>=0} is not a CFL.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram of CFL closure properties: CFLs closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, intersection with regular. NOT closed under intersection with another CFL or complement.

given.aim: Identify the operation that does not preserve the CFL property.

terms.meaning: The set of all strings formed by concatenating a string from L1 with a string from L2.
terms.connects: CFLs are closed under concatenation — always yields a CFL.

terms.meaning: Intersection of a CFL with a regular language.
terms.connects: CFLs are closed under intersection with regular languages — always yields a CFL.

terms.meaning: The set of strings in both L1 and L2.
terms.example: {a^n b^n c^m} ∩ {a^m b^n c^n} = {a^n b^n c^n} which is not a CFL.
terms.connects: CFLs are NOT closed under intersection — can yield a non-CFL.

given.plan: Check each operation against known CFL closure properties: CFLs are closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, and intersection with regular sets. CFLs are NOT closed under intersection of two CFLs or complement.

steps.title: Check CFL closure properties
steps.apply:
A. L1.L2: Concatenation — CFLs are closed under concatenation. Always a CFL.
B. L1 ∩ R: Intersection with regular — CFLs are closed under intersection with regular languages (proved using PDA + DFA product construction). Always a CFL.
C. L1 ∩ L2: Intersection of two CFLs — CFLs are NOT closed under intersection. Classic counterexample: {a^n b^n c^m | n,m >= 0} and {a^m b^n c^n | n,m >= 0} are both CFLs, but their intersection is {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 0} which requires a pushdown automaton to count three equal quantities simultaneously — this is not a CFL.
D. L1 U L2: Union — CFLs are closed under union. Always a CFL.
steps.note: The key non-closure result for CFLs is intersection of two CFLs and complement.

steps.title: Identify the answer
steps.apply: Only option C (L1 ∩ L2) is not necessarily a CFL. All other operations preserve the CFL property.
steps.note: This is a standard GATE fact: CFLs are NOT closed under intersection or complement.

solution.result: C. L1 ∩ L2 is not necessarily a context free language.

questions.formula_note: CFL closure properties: closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, intersection with regular; NOT closed under intersection with CFL or complement.

questions.question:
Define a context free language L_init(L) = {u | u is a prefix of some string in L} (in other words, init(L) is the set of all prefixes of strings in L).
Let L = {0^m 1^m | m >= 0}.
Then init(L) is:
A. the set of all binary strings with unequal number of 0's and 1's
B. the set of all binary strings including the null string
C. the set of all binary strings with exactly one more 1 than the number of 0's
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: What is init(L) when L = {0^m 1^m | m >= 0}

understand.plain: We are given the language L = {0^m 1^m | m >= 0}, which includes epsilon, 01, 0011, 000111, etc. The operation init(L) collects all prefixes of all strings in L. We need to figure out what set of strings forms the collection of all prefixes.

keywords.explain: A prefix of a string w is any string u such that w = uv for some string v (v may be empty). Every string is a prefix of itself.
The set of all prefixes of all strings in L. Also written as PREF(L).
keywords.example: If L = {ab}, then init(L) = {epsilon, a, ab}.

understand.visual_alt: The strings in L = {0^m 1^m} are: epsilon, 01, 0011, 000111, ... Their prefixes are: epsilon, 0, 01, 00, 001, 0011, 000, 0001, 00011, 000111, ... These are strings of the form 0^i 1^j where i >= j >= 0.

terms.meaning: Strings with equal number of 0s followed by equal number of 1s, including empty string (m=0).
terms.connects: The source language whose prefixes we compute.

terms.meaning: For a string 0^m 1^m, prefixes are: 0^m 1^j for 0 <= j <= m, and 0^i for 0 <= i <= m.
terms.connects: Taking all such prefixes over all m gives init(L).

terms.meaning: init(L) = {0^i 1^j | i >= j >= 0}, i.e., strings where number of 0s >= number of 1s >= 0, with all 0s before all 1s.
terms.connects: This characterization must be matched against the answer choices.

given.plan: Enumerate prefixes of each string in L. A prefix of 0^m 1^m is either 0^m 1^j (0 <= j <= m) i.e., all zeros plus some ones, or 0^i (a prefix of the zeros part). So init(L) = {0^i 1^j | i >= j, i,j >= 0}. Compare with the answer choices.

steps.title: Enumerate prefixes of strings in L
steps.formula_raw: init(L) = {u | exists w in L such that w = uv for some v}
steps.note: Crucially, we cannot have j > i (more 1s than 0s) in init(L), and all 1s must come after all 0s.

steps.title: Compare with answer choices
steps.apply:
A. 'Binary strings with unequal number of 0s and 1s' — this includes strings like 1, 10, 110 which are NOT in init(L). WRONG.
B. 'All binary strings including null string' — strings like 1, 10, 01100 etc. are not in init(L). WRONG.
C. 'Strings with exactly one more 1 than 0s' — this is a very small subset, not all of init(L). WRONG.
D. 'None of the above' — since init(L) = {0^i 1^j | i >= j >= 0}, which is none of the above descriptions. CORRECT.
steps.note: init(L) is a specific CFL: {0^i 1^j | i >= j >= 0}. None of options A, B, C describe it correctly.

solution.result: D. None of the above. init(L) = {0^i 1^j | i >= j >= 0}, the set of binary strings of the form 0...01...1 where the number of 0s is at least the number of 1s.

questions.formula_note: init(L) or PREF(L): CFLs are closed under prefix operation. For L={0^m 1^m}, prefixes are exactly strings 0^i 1^j with i >= j >= 0.

questions.question:
Context-free languages are closed under:
A. Union, intersection
B. Union, Kleene closure
C. Intersection, complement
D. Complement, Kleene closure
questions.to_find: Which pair of operations are CFLs closed under

understand.plain: We need to know which operations always produce a CFL when applied to CFLs. CFLs are closed under some operations (like union, concatenation, Kleene star) but NOT others (like intersection of two CFLs, or complement).

keywords.explain: L* is the set of all strings formed by concatenating zero or more strings from L. If L is a CFL, L* is also a CFL.
keywords.example: If L = {ab}, L* = {epsilon, ab, abab, ababab, ...}

keywords.explain: CFLs are closed under: union, concatenation, Kleene star, reversal, homomorphism, intersection with regular. NOT closed under: intersection of two CFLs, complement.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n} and {b^n c^n} are both CFLs but their intersection {a^n b^n c^n} is not.

understand.visual_alt: Table showing CFL closure: YES for union, concatenation, Kleene star; NO for intersection of two CFLs and complement.

given.aim: Identify the pair of operations under which CFLs are closed.

terms.meaning: L1 U L2 = set of strings in L1 or L2 (or both).
terms.example: If L1 and L2 are CFLs, build a new start symbol S -> S1 | S2.
terms.connects: CFLs are closed under union — always yields a CFL.

terms.example: If L is a CFL, L* is also a CFL (PDA can loop).
terms.connects: CFLs are closed under Kleene closure.

terms.meaning: Intersection of two CFLs or complement of a CFL.
terms.example: Intersection of {a^n b^n c^m} and {a^m b^n c^n} is {a^n b^n c^n} — not a CFL.
terms.connects: CFLs are NOT closed under intersection or complement.

given.plan: Eliminate options containing intersection or complement (since CFLs are not closed under these). The correct pair must be from {union, concatenation, Kleene star, intersection with regular}.

steps.title: Eliminate options with non-closure operations
steps.apply:
A. Union, intersection — intersection of two CFLs is not always a CFL. WRONG.
C. Intersection, complement — both fail. WRONG.
D. Complement, Kleene closure — complement fails. WRONG.
B. Union, Kleene closure — both are valid closure operations for CFLs. CORRECT.
steps.note: CFLs are closed under union (use two start symbols) and Kleene closure (PDA can be made to loop).

steps.title: Verify option B
steps.apply:
Union: Given CFLs L1 and L2 with grammars G1 (start S1) and G2 (start S2), create G with new start S and rules S -> S1 | S2. This generates L1 U L2, which is a CFL.
Kleene closure: Given CFL L with grammar G (start S), create G' with new start S' and rules S' -> SS' | epsilon. This generates L*, which is a CFL.
steps.note: Both constructions are standard and straightforward.

solution.result: B. Union, Kleene closure — context-free languages are closed under both operations.

questions.formula_note: CFL closure: closed under union (combine grammars), concatenation, Kleene star (loop production); NOT closed under intersection of two CFLs or complement.

questions.question:
Show that the language L = {xcx | x in {0,1}*, c is a terminal symbol} is not context free.
(Note: c is not 0 or 1. The language consists of strings where a binary word x appears twice separated by the special terminal symbol c.)
questions.answer: This is a proof/explanation question. L = {xcx | x in {0,1}*, c is a terminal symbol} is not context free (proved by Pumping Lemma for CFLs).
questions.to_find: Prove that L = {xcx | x in {0,1}*, c is a terminal symbol} is not a context free language

understand.plain: We have the language L = {xcx | x in {0,1}*, c is a special terminal not in {0,1}}. This language consists of all strings where the same binary string x appears on both sides of c. We need to show L is not a CFL using the Pumping Lemma for context free languages.

keywords.explain: For any CFL L, there exists a pumping length p such that any string s in L with |s| >= p can be written as s = uvwxy where |vwx| <= p, |vx| >= 1, and for all i >= 0, uv^i wx^i y is in L.
keywords.example: Used to prove {a^n b^n c^n} is not a CFL by showing no valid decomposition exists.

keywords.explain: The language of strings where the same binary string x appears twice separated by c. The key property is the exact copy of x on both sides.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='160' viewBox='0 0 520 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='140' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>0^p</text><rect x='155' y='20' width='40' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='175' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>c</text><rect x='200' y='20' width='140' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='270' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>0^p</text><text x='260' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>s = 0^p c 0^p, a string in L with |s| >= p</text><text x='260' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Any pumping of v and x cannot keep both halves equal</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: String s = 0^p c 0^p in L. The string has p zeros, then c, then p zeros. Pumping v and x from this string will either change the count of zeros on one or both sides, making the result not in L (the two halves of the string won't be equal anymore).

given.aim: Prove L = {xcx | x in {0,1}*, c is a terminal} is not a CFL using the Pumping Lemma.

terms.meaning: The constant guaranteed by the Pumping Lemma for CFLs. Any string in L of length >= p can be decomposed for pumping.
terms.example: For the proof, choose s = 0^p c 0^p which has length 2p+1 >= p.
terms.connects: The existence of p is the starting assumption we use to derive a contradiction.

terms.meaning: The pumping lemma guarantees a split where |vwx| <= p, |vx| >= 1, and all pumpings uv^i wx^i y stay in L.
terms.example: For s = 0^p c 0^p, vwx is a substring of length <= p.
terms.connects: We show no valid decomposition satisfies all pumping conditions.

terms.meaning: Our chosen string in L: x = 0^p, so s = 0^p c 0^p.
terms.connects: The key is that c separates equal halves; any pumping that affects only one half breaks equality.

given.plan: Assume L is a CFL. Apply Pumping Lemma with string s = 0^p c 0^p. Since |vwx| <= p < 2p+1, the substring vwx cannot span both the left 0^p and right 0^p (it cannot cross both sides past c). Case analysis shows pumping always produces a string not in L, giving contradiction.

steps.title: Assume L is CFL and choose pumping string
steps.formula_raw: Pumping Lemma: exists p > 0 such that for all s in L with |s| >= p, s = uvwxy with |vwx| <= p, |vx| >= 1, and for all i >= 0, uv^i wx^i y in L.
steps.apply: Assume for contradiction that L is a CFL. Let p be the pumping length. Choose s = 0^p c 0^p. Clearly s is in L (with x = 0^p) and |s| = 2p+1 >= p.
steps.note: The string s = 0^p c 0^p is a natural choice because its symmetry around c makes it hard to pump.

steps.title: Case analysis on where vwx lies in s
steps.apply:
Since |vwx| <= p and |s| = 2p+1, the substring vwx cannot overlap the first p zeros AND the last p zeros (those are separated by distance p+1 including c). Cases:

Case 1: vwx lies entirely in the left 0^p (before c). Then v and x are strings of 0s. Pumping i=2 gives 0^(p+|v|+|x|) c 0^p. Now the left side has more 0s than right, so the two halves are unequal. Not in L. Contradiction.

Case 2: vwx lies entirely in the right 0^p (after c). Then pumping i=2 gives 0^p c 0^(p+|v|+|x|). Right side has more 0s than left. Not in L. Contradiction.

Case 3: vwx contains the c symbol. Since |vwx| <= p, it can contain at most one c (there is only one c). Pumping i=2 would produce two c's, giving a string of the form ...c...c... which cannot be in L (L has exactly one c). Contradiction.

Case 4: vwx spans some 0s from left AND c AND some 0s from right. Pumping i=0 (removing v and x) removes some 0s from both sides but not necessarily equal numbers, breaking the equal-halves requirement. Or pumping i=2 duplicates the c plus surrounding zeros unevenly. Either way, the result is not in L.
steps.note: In all cases, pumping produces a string not in L, contradicting the Pumping Lemma.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Since every possible decomposition s = uvwxy with |vwx| <= p and |vx| >= 1 leads to a pumped string not in L, the Pumping Lemma is violated. Therefore, our assumption that L is a CFL must be false.
steps.note: L = {xcx | x in {0,1}*, c terminal} is not a context free language.

solution.result: By the Pumping Lemma for CFLs, choosing s = 0^p c 0^p in L, any decomposition s = uvwxy with |vwx| <= p and |vx| >= 1 leads to a pumped string outside L. Therefore L is not context free.

questions.formula_note: Pumping Lemma for CFLs: for any CFL, strings longer than the pumping length p can be decomposed as uvwxy with |vwx| <= p, |vx| >= 1, and all pumpings uv^i wx^i y in L. A violation proves non-CFL-ness.
